perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Each party may disclose th e Confidential Information of the other party in the following circumstances: (a) with the prior written cons ent of the other party; (b) to its employees, professional advisors, affiliates, consultants, shareholders, financiers and authorised representa tives but only to the extent that disclosure is necessary for the purpose; or (c) where disclosure is required by law, by a court of competen t jurisdiction, by the rules of any stock exchange or by another appropriate regulatory body, provided that all reasonable steps to pr event such disclosure shall be taken, the disclosure shall be of the minimum amount re quired, and the Receiving Party consults the Disclosing Party first on the proposed form, timing, nature and purpose of the disclosure with as much prior notice as is practicable in the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Each party may disclose th e Sensitive Information of the other party in the following circumstances: (a) with the prior written cons ent of the other party; (b) to its employees, professional advisors, affiliates, consultants, shareholders, financiers and authorised representa tives but only to the extent that disclosure is necessary for the purpose; or (c) where disclosure is required by law, by a court of competen t jurisdiction, by the rules of any stock exchange or by another appropriate regulatory body, provided that all reasonable steps to pr event such disclosure shall be taken, the disclosure shall be of the minimum amount re quired, and the Receiving Party consults the Disclosing Party first on the proposed form, timing, nature and purpose of the disclosure with as much prior notice as is practicable in the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' in subsection 4.1 creates confusion regarding the scope of permissible disclosure. The agreement uses the defined term 'Confidential Information' elsewhere. This introduces a contradiction because it is unclear whether the disclosure rules in 4.1 now apply to something different than defined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 Each party may disclose th e Sensitive Information of the other party in the following circumstances: (a) with the prior written cons ent of the other party; (b) to its employees, professional advisors, affiliates, consultants, shareholders, financiers and authorised representa tives but only to the extent that disclosure is necessary for the purpose; or (c) where disclosure is required by law, by a court of competen t jurisdiction, by the rules of any stock exchange or by another appropriate regulatory body, provided that all reasonable steps to pr event such disclosure shall be taken, the disclosure shall be of the minimum amount re quired, and the Receiving Party consults the Disclosing Party first on the proposed form, timing, nature and purpose of the disclosure with as much prior notice as is practicable in the circumstances.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' in section 4.1 creates a contradiction. The contract elsewhere uses the defined term 'Confidential Information'. The explanation states that this change introduces uncertainty because it becomes unclear whether the disclosure rules in 4.1 apply to something different than defined in the original context, resulting in a Misaligned Terminology contradiction, where 'Sensitive Information' is used interchangeably with 'Confidential Information' without defining it.

perturbation.original_text: 5 Exceptions 5.1 This Agreement does not apply to Confidential Information: (a) to the extent it is or becomes generally available to the public other than through a breach of this Agreement; (b) which the Receiving Party can show by it s written or other records was in its lawful possession prior to receipt from the Disclosi ng Party and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confidence; (c) which subsequently comes into the posse ssion of the Receiving Party from a third party who does not owe the Disclo sing Party an oblig ation of confidence in relation to it; or (d) which has been independently develo ped by the Receivi ng Party without reference to the Disclosing Part y’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 5 Exceptions 5.1 This Agreement does not apply to Protected Information: (a) to the extent it is or becomes generally available to the public other than through a breach of this Agreement; (b) which the Receiving Party can show by it s written or other records was in its lawful possession prior to receipt from the Disclosi ng Party and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confidence; (c) which subsequently comes into the posse ssion of the Receiving Party from a third party who does not owe the Disclo sing Party an oblig ation of confidence in relation to it; or (d) which has been independently develo ped by the Receivi ng Party without reference to the Disclosing Part y’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Information' in the first line of clause 5.1 creates a conflict. While the rest of clause 5.1(d) refers to 'Confidential Information', the initial statement excludes 'Protected Information'. This leads to ambiguity about whether information independently developed, using the original Confidential Information, still falls under the exceptions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5 Exceptions 5.1 This Agreement does not apply to Protected Information: (a) to the extent it is or becomes generally available to the public other than through a breach of this Agreement; (b) which the Receiving Party can show by it s written or other records was in its lawful possession prior to receipt from the Disclosi ng Party and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confidence; (c) which subsequently comes into the posse ssion of the Receiving Party from a third party who does not owe the Disclo sing Party an oblig ation of confidence in relation to it; or (d) which has been independently develo ped by the Receivi ng Party without reference to the Disclosing Part y’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Information' in clause 5.1 creates a direct contradiction with the continued use of 'Confidential Information' later in the same clause, specifically in section (d). The contradicted text is in the same location as the changed text, but the initial change impacts the overall meaning of the section. Since the clause now refers to both 'Protected Information' and 'Confidential Information', it is unclear which type of information the exceptions apply to, creating a clear conflict and ambiguity as described in the Perturbation Explanation, which is a Misaligned Terminology type of In-Text Contradiction, thus aligning with the Perturbation Definitions and requiring a "YES" response.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or (b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Sensitive Data and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or (b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Data' in clause 8.1(a), where the party undertakes to return information, creates a contradiction. Part (a) now requires the return of 'Sensitive Data', while parts (b) and the rest of the clause still specify the destruction of 'Confidential Information'. It is unclear which information should be returned versus destroyed, particularly if 'Sensitive Data' and 'Confidential Information' are not the same.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.1 Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Sensitive Data and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or (b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Data' in clause 8.1(a) creates a contradiction because the rest of clause 8.1, including part (b), still refers to 'Confidential Information'. The explanation provided highlights that it's now unclear whether parties should return 'Sensitive Data' and destroy 'Confidential Information', particularly if the two terms are not synonymous. This misalignment in terminology directly leads to an in-text contradiction, as per the definition, creating conflicting obligations within the same clause. The contradicted text is the same clause which now contains the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any claim for lo sses under this Agreement sha ll be restricted to direct loss only and shall not extend to indirect or cons equential loss.
perturbation.changed_text: Any claim for lo sses under this Agreement sha ll be restricted to direct loss only and shall not extend to secondary or cons equential loss.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'indirect' to 'secondary' in the sentence regarding losses creates a contradiction. 'Secondary' is not defined, whereas 'direct' and 'consequential' are commonly understood legal terms, so it creates confusion, especially since 'consequential' is still present in the sentence. It contradicts the understanding of what is considered a loss under the terms of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: It is acknowledged that damages would no t be an adequate remedy for a breach of this Agreement and each party is entitled to the re medy of injunction, specific performance and other equitable relief for a threatened or actual breach of this Agreement. Any claim for lo sses under this Agreement sha ll be restricted to direct loss only and shall not extend to secondary or cons equential loss.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'indirect' loss, a commonly understood legal term, alongside 'consequential' loss. The changed text replaces 'indirect' with 'secondary', which is undefined. This introduces ambiguity as to what constitutes a loss under the agreement. The Contradicted Text contains the same modified clause, which includes the unclear term 'secondary', creating an in-text contradiction. This is because 'secondary' is not defined, and replacing a clear legal term with an ambiguous one contradicts the established understanding of loss within the agreement, making it difficult to interpret and enforce.
